---
title: "TAMBAPANNI: A History Without Borders"
subtitle: "Reconstructing the Identity of the Island Known Today as Sri Lanka"
date: 2026-03-01
author:
  - name: Ananthaneshan Elampoornan
    orcid: 0009-0004-9181-2270
google-scholar: true
citation:
  type: article
  container-title: "Tambapanni White Paper Series"
  issued: 2026-03
  url: https://tambapanni.github.io/papers/history.html
  pdf-url: https://tambapanni.github.io/papers/pdf/1. Tambapanni_History_Without_Borders.pdf
  doi: 10.5072/zenodo.471074
categories: [political-economy, state-building, historiography, sri-lanka]
abstract: |
  Every history is written through a lens. The dominant historiography of the island known today as Sri Lanka has been written through three overlapping lenses that systematically distort the evidence: the post-Westphalian nation-state framework, the Germanic Aryan philological tradition, and the colonial administrative need for fixed ethnic categories. A modern democratic state rests on three institutional pillars: an effective and impartial state apparatus; the rule of law that binds even the most powerful actors; and democratic accountability through which citizens hold the state to account. The critical word is impartial. Sri Lanka democratized in 1931 before building a professional state apparatus, creating a sequencing failure where democratic competition became ethnic patronage competition. The Sinhala Only Act of 1956 and subsequent communal violence were not failures of democracy but the logical outputs of a democratic system operating without an impartial state to constrain what democracy could do.
---

## Part 1 of the Tambapanni Series

**Status:** Complete · v1.19 · March 2026

### Key Arguments

- **The Sinhalese-Tamil division was manufactured in the 19th century, not ancient** The division was constructed through colonial administration, Orientalist philology, and competitive nationalist mobilization, not primordial ethnic incompatibility.

- **Sri Lanka democratized before building an impartial state apparatus** Universal suffrage arrived in 1931 without the institutional substrate that constrains democratic competition, leading to ethnic patronage politics.

- **The Vijaya origin narrative places his departure from Gujarat, not Bengal** The Bengal reading is a 19th-century philological error by Geiger; the Mahavamsa text specifies Lala country, which geographic evidence places on the Gujarat coast.

- **The ethnic conflict is structural, not communal** The problems stem from sequencing failure in state-building, not ancient ethnic hatred between two irreconcilable peoples.

- **The Mahavamsa was institutional legitimacy document, not ethnic manifesto** It was written by Mahavihara monks in Pali to establish their interpretive monopoly over Buddhism against rival Abhayagiri, not to create popular ethnic identity.

- **Pre-colonial identity was connective and maritime, not ethnically bounded** The island operated as one civilization with internal diversity, exemplified by the four Telugu Hindu Nayak kings who became the greatest patrons of Sinhalese Buddhism.

### Frameworks Applied

- **Fukuyama Sequencing** — The order of institutional development matters: impartial state apparatus must precede democratization to avoid patronage capture

### Downloads

[Download PDF](pdf/1.%20Tambapanni_History_Without_Borders.pdf){.btn .btn-primary} [DOI: 10.5072/zenodo.471074](https://doi.org/10.5072/zenodo.471074){.btn .btn-outline-secondary}

### Citation

Elampoornan, A. (2026). Tambapanni: A History Without Borders, v1.17. orcid.org/0009-0004-9181-2270

---

[← Back to series overview](../index.qmd)
